{{< include /preamble/commands.qmd >}}

# Common Stochastic Variables
## Bernoulli {#sec-Bernoulli_Stochastic_Variable}
A bernoulli stochastic variable $X\sim\Ber{n, p}$ has the sample space $\Omega = \{0, 1\}$ with the probabilities $\Pr{X = 1} = p$ and the opposite, $\Pr{X = 0} = 1 - p$.

### Distribution {.unnumbered}
$$
\begin{cases}
    p &\textrm{if } x = 1 \\
    1 - p &\textrm{if } x = 0
\end{cases}
$$

### Expected Value {.unnumbered}
$$
\E{X} = p
$$

### Variance {.unnumbered}
$$
\Var{X} = p \cdot (1 - p)
$$


## Binomial {#sec-Binomial_Stochastic_Variable}
A binomial stochastic variable is a Bernoulli stochastic variable repeated $n$ times.

### Distribution {.unnumbered}
The PMF (see @sec-PMF) is given as
$$
\binom{n}{k}\cdot p^k \cdot \left(1 - p\right)^{n - k}
$$
where $k$ is the number of $1$'s out of the possible $n$ experiments.

### Expected Value {.unnumbered}
$$
\E{X} = n \cdot p
$$

### Variance {.unnumbered}
$$
\Var{X} = n \cdot p \cdot (1 - p)
$$


## Geometric {#sec-Geometric_Stochastic_Variable}
The geometric distribution models the number of independent Bernoulli trials needed to obtain the first success, where each trial succeeds with probability $p$. It is the discrete analogue of the exponential distribution and shares the memoryless property.

### Distribution {.unnumbered}
{{< include /Figures/Probability/Geometric_Distribution.qmd >}}

### Expected Value {.unnumbered}
$$
\E{X} = \frac{1}{p}
$$

### Variance {.unnumbered}
$$
\Var{X} = \frac{1 - p}{p^2}
$$


## Negative Binomial {#sec-Negative_Binomial_Stochastic_Variable}
Consider an experiment of independent Bernoulli (see @sec-Bernoulli_Stochastic_Variable) trials, each with probability $p$. If we are interested in when we have $r$ successes consider the Negative Binomial distribution
$$
X \sim \NegBin{r, p}
$$

### Distribution {.unnumbered}
{{< include /Figures/Probability/Negative_Binomial_Distribution.qmd >}}

PMF
$$
p_X(k) = \binom{k - 1}{r - 1}p^r\left(1 - p\right)^{k - r}\quad
k = r, r+1, \dots
$$

### Expected Value {.unnumbered}
$$
\E{X} = \frac{r}{p}
$$

### Variance {.unnumbered}
$$
\Var{X} = r \frac{\left(1 - p\right)}{p^2}
$$


## Hypergeometric {#sec-Hypergeometric_Stochastic_Variable}
The hypergeometric distribution models the number of successes in a sample drawn without replacement from a finite population containing a fixed number of successes. It is used in scenarios such as quality control or card games where the order of selection matters and the population is not infinite.

### Distribution {.unnumbered}
{{< include /Figures/Probability/Hypergeometric_Distribution.qmd >}}

$$
\frac{\binom{m}{y} \binom{N - m}{n - y}}{\binom{N}{n}}
$$

### Expected Value {.unnumbered}
$$
\E{X} = n \cdot \frac{m}{N}
$$

### Variance {.unnumbered}
$$
\Var{X} = n \cdot \frac{m}{N} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{m}{N}\right) \cdot \frac{N - n}{N - 1}
$$


## Poisson {#sec-Poisson_Stochastic_Variable}
The Poisson distribution models the number of events occurring in a fixed interval of time or space, given that events occur independently at a constant average rate $\lambda > 0$. It is often used for count data such as the number of arrivals per hour or defects per unit.

$X \sim \Pd{\lambda}$, $\lambda > 0$, observations are independent, and $\log{\lambda}$ is a linear function of $x$.

### Distribution {.unnumbered}
{{< include /Figures/Probability/Poisson_Distribution.qmd >}}

The PMF (see @sec-PMF) is given as
$$
p_X(x) = \frac{\lambda^x}{x!}\exp{-\lambda}
$$

### Expected Value {.unnumbered}
$$
\E{X} = \lambda
$$

### Variance {.unnumbered}
$$
\Var{X} = \lambda
$$


## Exponential {#sec-Exponential_Stochastic_Variable}
The exponential distribution models the time between events in a Poisson process, i.e., the waiting time until the next independent event occurs at a constant rate $\lambda > 0$. It has the memoryless property: the probability of waiting an additional time $t$ does not depend on how long one has already waited.

### Distribution {.unnumbered}
{{< include /Figures/Probability/Exponential_Distribution.qmd >}}

$$
f_X(x) = \begin{cases}
    \lambda \cdot \exp{-\lambda \cdot x} & x > 0 \\
    0 & x \leq 0
\end{cases}
$$

### Expected Value {.unnumbered}
$$
\E{X} = \frac{1}{\lambda}
$$

### Variance {.unnumbered}
$$
\Var{X} = \frac{1}{\lambda^2}
$$


## Gamma {#sec-Gamma_Stochastic_Variable}
The gamma distribution is a flexible two-parameter family of continuous distributions that generalizes the exponential distribution. It models the waiting time until the $\alpha$-th event in a Poisson process, and includes the chi-square and exponential distributions as special cases. $X \sim \Gamd{\alpha, \beta}$, where $\alpha > 0$ is the shape parameter and $\beta > 0$ is the rate parameter.

### Distribution {.unnumbered}
{{< include /Figures/Probability/Gamma_Distribution.qmd >}}

$X \sim \Gamd{\alpha, \beta}$
$$
f_X(x) = \begin{cases}
    \frac{\beta^\alpha}{\varGamma(\alpha)} x^{\alpha - 1}\exp{-\beta \cdot x} & x > 0\\
    0 & x \leq 0
\end{cases}
$$

### Expected Value {.unnumbered}
$$
\E{X} = \frac{\alpha}{\beta}
$$

### Variance {.unnumbered}
$$
\Var{X} = \frac{\alpha}{\beta^2}
$$


## Gaussian {#sec-Gaussian_Stochastic_Variable}
The Gaussian distribution is the most widely used continuous distribution, characterized by its symmetric bell-shaped curve. It arises naturally in many settings due to the central limit theorem, which states that sums of independent random variables tend toward a Gaussian distribution. $X \sim \Nd{\mu, \sigma^2}$, with the standard Gaussian given as $X \sim \Nd{0, 1}$.

### Distribution {.unnumbered}
{{< include /Figures/Probability/Gaussian_Distribution.qmd >}}

The PDF (see @sec-PDF) is given as
$$
f_X(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \cdot \pi} \cdot \sigma} \cdot \exp{- \frac{1}{2 \cdot \sigma^2}\left(x - \mu\right)^2}
$$

### Expected Value {.unnumbered}
$$
\E{X} = \mu
$$

### Variance {.unnumbered}
$$
\Var{X} = \sigma^2
$$


## Beta {#sec-Beta_Stochastic_Variable}
The beta distribution is a continuous distribution defined on the interval $[0, 1]$, making it well-suited for modeling probabilities or proportions. It is parameterized by two positive shape parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$ that control the shape of the distribution, ranging from uniform to strongly skewed or U-shaped.

### Distribution {.unnumbered}
{{< include /Figures/Probability/Beta_Distribution.qmd >}}

### Expected Value {.unnumbered}
$$
\E{X} = \frac{\alpha}{\alpha + \beta}
$$

### Variance {.unnumbered}
$$
\Var{X} = \frac{\alpha \beta}{(\alpha + \beta)^2 (\alpha + \beta + 1)}
$$


## Uniform {#sec-Uniform_Stochastic_Variable}
The uniform distribution assigns equal probability to all values within a bounded interval $[a, b]$. It is the simplest continuous distribution and is commonly used as a non-informative prior or for generating random samples via inverse transform sampling. $X \sim \Ud{a, b}$.

### Distribution {.unnumbered}
$$
f_X(x) = \begin{cases}
    \frac{1}{b - a} & x \in [a, b]\\
    0 & x \notin [a,b]
\end{cases}
$$
$$
F_X(x) = \begin{cases}
    0 & x < a\\
    \frac{x - a}{b - a} & a \leq x \leq b\\
    1 & x > b
\end{cases}
$$


### Expected Value {.unnumbered}
$$
\E{X} = \frac{1}{2}\left(a + b\right)
$$

### Variance {.unnumbered}
$$
\Var{X} = \frac{1}{12}\left(b - a\right)^2
$$


## Chi-square {#sec-Chi-square_Stochastic_Variable}
The chi-square distribution is a special case of the gamma distribution and arises as the distribution of the sum of squares of $k$ independent standard Gaussian random variables. It is widely used in hypothesis testing (e.g., goodness-of-fit tests) and in the construction of confidence intervals for variance. $X \sim \Gamd{\frac{k}{2}, \frac{1}{2}}$, $k \in \N$.

### Distribution {.unnumbered}
{{< include /Figures/Probability/Chi-square_Distribution.qmd >}}

$$
f_X(x) = \begin{cases}
    \frac{x^{\frac{k}{2} - 1}\exp{-\frac{x}{2}}}{2^{\frac{k}{2} \cdot \varGamma\left(\frac{k}{2}\right)}} & x > 0 \\
    0 & x \leq 0
\end{cases}
$$

### Expected Value {.unnumbered}
$$
\E{X} = k
$$

### Variance {.unnumbered}
$$
\Var{X} = 2 \cdot k
$$


## F {#sec-F_Stochastic_Variable}
### Distribution {.unnumbered}
### Expected Value {.unnumbered}
### Variance {.unnumbered}


## T {#sec-T_Stochastic_Variable}
### Distribution {.unnumbered}
$$
\frac{\Gamma\left(\frac{d + 1}{2}\right)}{\sqrt{\pi d} \cdot \Gamma\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{x^2}{d}\right)^{-\frac{d + 1}{2}}
$$
$d > 0$ is the degrees of freedom.

### Expected Value {.unnumbered}
$$
\E{X} = \begin{cases}
    0 & d > 1\\
    \textrm{undefined} & d \leq 1
\end{cases}
$$

### Variance {.unnumbered}
$$
\Var{X} = \begin{cases}
    \frac{d}{d - 2} & d > 2 \\
    \infty & 1 < d \leq 2 \\
    \textrm{undefined} & d \leq 1
\end{cases}
$$
$$
